revision.text:
[[ar:الإمبراطور بيداتسو]][[arz:الامبراطور بيداتسو]][[azb:بیداتسو (ایمپیراتور)]][[cs:Bidacu]][[de:Bidatsu]][[en:Emperor Bidatsu]][[es:Bidatsu Tennō]][[fa:امپراتور بیداتسو]][[fi:Bidatsu]][[fr:Bidatsu]][[id:Kaisar Bidatsu]][[it:Bidatsu]][[ja:敏達天皇]][[ka:ბიდაცუ]][[ko:비다쓰 천황]][[mr:बिदात्सु]][[nl:Bidatsu]][[pl:Bidatsu]][[pt:Bidatsu]][[ru:Император Бидацу]][[sh:Car Bidatsu]][[simple:Emperor Bidatsu]][[sl:Cesar Bidacu]][[sv:Bidatsu]][[th:จักรพรรดิบิดัตสึ]][[tl:Emperador Bidatsu]][[tr:Bidatsu]][[uk:Імператор Бідацу]][[uz:Imperator Bidatsu]][[vi:Thiên hoàng Bidatsu]][[yo:Emperor Bidatsu]][[zh-min-nan:Bidatu Thian-hông]][[zh-yue:敏達天皇]][[zh:敏达天皇]] {{Infobox person|image=File:Emperor Bidatsu.jpg|birth_date=0538|death_date={{death date|0585|9|14}}|death_place={{ill|Sakurai|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|ruler|qid=Q1097498|1=ruler}}|title={{ill|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|1=Emperor of Japan}}|spouse={{ill|Ominako no Iratsume|qid=Q97342104|1=Ominako no Iratsume}}; {{ill|Princess Hiro Hime|qid=Q11064567|1=Hirohime}}; {{ill|Suiko|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}}|children={{ill|Nukate-hime|qid=Q11604780|1=Nukate-hime}}; {{ill|Owari no Miko|qid=Q89517460|1=Owari no Miko}}; {{ill|Prince Kasuga|qid=Q11513657|1=Prince Kasuga}}; {{ill|Prince Naniha|qid=Q11659560|1=Prince Naniha}}; {{ill|Prince Ohomata|qid=Q11437415|1=Prince Ohomata}}; {{ill|Prince Oshisaka-no-Hikohito-no-Ōe|qid=Q11497661|1=Prince Oshisaka Hikohito no Ōe}}; {{ill|Prince Takeda|qid=Q11599359|1=Prince Takeda}}; {{ill|Prince Wohari|qid=Q11620912|1=Prince Wohari}}; {{ill|Princess Tame|qid=Q11577210|1=Princess Tame}}; {{ill|Princess Uji no Shitsukahi|qid=Q10509070|1=Princess Uji no Shitsukahi}}; {{ill|Princess Uji no Shitsukai|qid=Q97342128|1=Princess Uji no Shitsukai}}; {{ill|Sakurai no Yumihari|qid=Q92923028|1=Sakurai no Yumihari}}; {{ill|Woharida|qid=Q11459566|1=Woharida}}|father={{ill|Kinmei|qid=Q329792|1=Emperor Kinmei}}|mother={{ill|Ishihime|qid=Q11585498|1=Princess Ishi-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Anahobe no Hashihito no Himemiko|qid=Q11597250|1=Princess Hashihito no Anahobe}}; {{ill|Imperial Prince Amatsukabe Anahobe|qid=Q11597248|1=Prince Anahobe}}; {{ill|Imperial Prince Iso no Kami Be|qid=Q11584775|1=Imperial Prince Iso no Kami Be}}; {{ill|Imperial Prince Iso no Kami|qid=Q11584769|1=Imperial Prince Iso no Kami}}; {{ill|Imperial Prince Katsuraki|qid=Q11620913|1=Imperial Prince Katsuraki}}; {{ill|Imperial Prince Sakurawi|qid=Q11537858|1=Imperial Prince Sakurai}}; {{ill|Suiko|qid=Q298057|1=Empress Suiko}}; {{ill|Sushun|qid=Q349238|1=Emperor Sushun}}; {{ill|Yōmei|qid=Q349281|1=Emperor Yōmei}}|family={{ill|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}|native_name=敏達天皇|resting_place={{ill|Taishi Nishiyama Kofun|qid=Q48751982|1=Taishi Nishiyama Kofun}}}} {{Short description|30th Emperor of Japan (reigned 572-585)|qq=}} {{Use dmy dates|date=April 2021|qq=}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Emperor Bidatsu
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|敏達天皇|qq=}}|qq=}} | title = [[Okimi|Great King of Yamato]] | succession = {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} | image = | caption = | reign = 30 April 572 – 14 September 585 | coronation = | cor-type = Japan | predecessor = {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} | successor = {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|qid=Q349281|lt=Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}} | posthumous name = [[Posthumous name#Chinese-style (Han-style) shigō|Chinese-style ''shigō'']]:
Emperor Bidatsu ({{lang|ja|敏達天皇|qq=}})

[[Posthumous name#Japanese-style shigō|Japanese-style ''shigō'']]:
Nunakuranofutotamashiki no Sumeramikoto ({{lang|ja|渟中倉太珠敷天皇|qq=}}) | spouses = [[Hirohime]]
[[:en:Nukatabe|Nukatabe]] (later Empress Suiko) | issue = See [[#Genealogy|below]] | royal house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} | father = {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}} | mother = {{ill|Princess Ishi-hime|en|Princess Ishi-hime|qid=Q11585498|lt=Princess Ishi-hime|1=Princess Ishi-hime}} | religion = [[Shinto]] | birth_date = 538 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date and age|585|9|14|538|df=yes|qq=}} | death_place = | burial_place = {{Nihongo||河内磯長中尾陵|''Kawachi no Shinaga no naka no o no misasagi''|qq=}} (Osaka) }} {{Nihongo|'''Emperor Bidatsu'''|敏達天皇|Bidatsu-tennō|extra=538 – 14 September 585|qq=}} was the 30th {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}},{{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} (''Kunaichō''), [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/ryobo/guide/030/index.html 敏達天皇 (30)]; retrieved 2013-1-31. according to the {{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=traditional order of succession|1=List of emperors of Japan}}.[[:en:Delmer M. Brown|Brown, Delmer]]. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA262 ''Gukanshō,'' pp. 262–263]; Varley, H. Paul. (1980). Jinnō Shōtōki. pp. 124–125; {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac|1=Isaac Titsingh}}. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon%20o%20dai%20itsi%20ran&pg=PA36 ''Annales des empereurs du Japon'', pp. 36–37]; {{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2002). "Traditional order of Tennō" in ''Japan encyclopedia,'' pp. 962–963. The years of reign of Bidatsu start in {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=572 and end in 585|1=Asuka period}}; however, there are no certain dates for this emperor's life or reign.Nussbaum, [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC&pg=PA962 "Traditional order of Tennō" at pp. 962–963]; excerpt, "dates ... should be treated with caution up to Emperor Bidatsu Tennō, the thirtieth on the list." The names and sequence of the early emperors were not confirmed as "traditional" until the reign of [[Emperor Kanmu]], who was the 50th {{ill|Monarch|en|Monarch|qid=Q116|lt=Monarch|1=Monarch}} of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial dynasty|1=Imperial House of Japan}}.[[William George Aston|Aston, William George]]. (1896). [https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA109 ''Nihongi,'' p. 109 n1]. ==Traditional narrative== Historians consider details about the life of Emperor Bidatsu to be possibly legendary, but probable.Kelly, Charles F. [http://www.t-net.ne.jp/~keally/kofun.html "Kofun Culture"], [http://www.t-net.ne.jp/~keally/index.htm ''Japanese Archaeology''.] 27 April 2009; retrieved 2013-1-31. The name Bidatsu''-tennō'' was created for him {{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|qid=Q696100|lt=posthumously|1=Posthumous name}} by later generations. In the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', he is called {{Nihongo|Nunakura no Futotamashiki|渟中倉太珠敷|qq=}}. His palace in {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} was called Osada no Miya of Iware. ==Events of Bidatsu's life== In the 15th year of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}’s reign, Bidatsu was named Crown Prince. In the {{Nihongo|32nd year of Kimmei''-tennō'''s reign|欽明天皇32nd year |extra=572|qq=}}, the old Emperor died, and the succession was received by his second son. Soon after, Emperor Bidatsu is said to have acceded to the throne.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon%20o%20dai%20itsi%20ran&pg=PA36 p. 36]; Varley, p. 44; n.b., the distinct act of ''senso'' is unrecognized prior to {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}; and all sovereigns except {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}, {{ill|Emperor Yōzei|en|Emperor Yōzei|qid=Q349280|lt=Yōzei|1=Emperor Yōzei}}, {{ill|Emperor Go-Toba|en|Emperor Go-Toba|qid=Q316658|lt=Go-Toba|1=Emperor Go-Toba}}, and {{ill|Emperor Fushimi|en|Emperor Fushimi|qid=Q373949|lt=Fushimi|1=Emperor Fushimi}} have ''senso'' and ''sokui'' in the same year until the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|qid=Q440447|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami|1=Emperor Go-Murakami}}; compare {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} (''Kunaichō''), [http://www.kunaicho.go.jp/e-about/seido/seido06-ph.html Ceremony of Accession (''Sokui-no-Rei'')]; retrieved 2013-1-31. Bidatsu's contemporary title would not have been ''tennō'', as most historians believe this title was not introduced until the reigns of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|qid=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} and {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|qid=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}}. Rather, it was presumably {{Nihongo|''Sumeramikoto'' or ''Amenoshita Shiroshimesu Ōkimi''|治天下大王|qq=}}, meaning "the great king who rules all under heaven". Alternatively, Bidatsu might have been referred to as {{Nihongo2|ヤマト大王/大君|qq=}} or the "Great King of Yamato". Bidatsu's reign was marked by power struggles about Buddhism. The two most important men in the court of Bidatsu were [[Soga no Umako]] and {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|qid=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon%20o%20dai%20itsi%20ran&pg=PA36 p. 36]. Soga supported the growth of Buddhism, and Moriya wanted to stop it.Brown, [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA262 pp. 262–263]. Bidatsu sought to re-establish relations with {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}n Kingdoms and, according to ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', his court successfully established relations with [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, two of the {{ill|Three Kingdoms of Korea|en|Three Kingdoms of Korea|qid=Q165292|lt=Three Kingdoms of Korea|1=Three Kingdoms of Korea}}. The Emperor died from a disease which afflicted him with sores, apparently the first royal victim of smallpox in Japan.Hopkins, Donald R. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=z2zMKsc1Sn0C&pg=PA106 ''The Greatest Killer'', p. 106], citing Aston (1896). [https://books.google.com/books?id=66aTvdFOqs0C&dq=Emperor%27s+disease+having+become+more+and+more+inveterate%2C+he+died+in+the+Great&pg=RA1-PA104 ''Nihongi'', Vol. II. p. 104]. The actual site of Bidatsu's {{ill|Grave|en|Grave|qid=Q173387|lt=grave|1=Grave}} is known. The Emperor is traditionally venerated at a {{ill|Memorial|en|Memorial|qid=Q5003624|lt=Memorial|1=Memorial}} [[Shinto]] [[shrine]] (''misasagi'') at Osaka. The {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|qid=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates this location as Bidatsu's {{ill|Mausoleum|en|Mausoleum|qid=Q162875|lt=Mausoleum|1=Mausoleum}}. It is formally named ''Kawachi no Shinaga no naka no o no misasagi''.{{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959) ''The Imperial House of Japan'', p. 419. ==Genealogy== He was the second son of {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}. His mother, Ishi-hime, was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|qid=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}}.Brown, [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&pg=PA262 p. 262]. Although he had many children, none of them would ever become Emperor.Ponsonby-Fane, p. 46. According to ''{{ill|Gukanshō|en|Gukanshō|qid=Q1368208|lt=Gukanshō|1=Gukanshō}}'', Bidatsu had four empresses and 16 Imperial children (6 sons and 10 daughters). Bidatsu's first empress, Hirohime, died in the fifth year of his reign. To replace her, he elevated one of his consorts, Princess Nukatabe, to the Rank of empress. Nukatabe was his half-sister by their father Kinmei. Later she ascended to the throne in her own right and is today known as {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}. He was succeeded first by one of his brothers, {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|qid=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}, then by another, {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|qid=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}}, and then Empress Suiko, his sister and wife, before his grandson, {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}}, eventually took the throne. *Empress: {{Nihongo|[[Hirohime]]|広姫|extra=d.575|qq=}}, Prince Okinaga-no-Mate's daughter **First Son: {{Nihongo|Prince Oshisako no Hikohito no Ōe|押坂彦人大兄皇子|extra=b.556|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Sakanobori|逆登皇女|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Uji|菟道皇女|qq=}}, [[Saiō]] *Empress: {{Nihongo|Princess Nukatabe|額田部皇女|qq=}}, later {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}, {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}'s daughter **{{Nihongo|Princess Uji no Kaitako|菟道貝蛸皇女|extra=b.570|qq=}}, married to {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} **{{Nihongo|Prince Takeda|竹田皇子|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Oharita|小墾田皇|extra=b.572|qq=}}, married to Prince Oshisako-no-Hikohito-no-Ōe **{{Nihongo|Princess Umori|鸕鶿守皇女|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|{{ill|Prince Kazuraki|ja|葛城王 (敏達天皇の子)|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Kazuraki|1=Prince Wohari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11620912}}|葛城王|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Prince Owari|尾張皇子|qq=}}, father of Tachibana-no-Oiratsume ({{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}'s consort) **{{Nihongo|Princess Tame|田眼皇女|qq=}}, married to {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|qid=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Sakurai no Yumihari|桜井弓張皇女|qq=}}, married to Prince Oshisako-no-Hikohito-no-Ōe, later married to Prince Kume ([[:en:Emperor Yomei|Emperor Yomei]]'s son) *Consort: {{Nihongo|Kasuga-no-Ominako-no-Iratsume|春日老女子|qq=}}, Kasuga no Nakakimi no Omi's daughter **{{Nihongo|Prince Naniwa|難波皇子|extra=560-587|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Prince Kasuga|春日皇子|extra=560-615|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Kuwata|桑田皇女|qq=}} **Third Son: {{Nihongo|Prince Ohomata|大派皇子|extra=b.585|qq=}} *Concubine: {{Nihongo|Unako no Otoshi|菟名子|qq=}}, Ohoka no Obito no Okuma's daughter **{{Nihongo|Princess Futohime|太姫|qq=}}, also {{Nihongo2|桜井皇女|qq=}} **{{Nihongo|Princess Nukatehime|糠手姫皇女|extra=570-664|qq=}}, married to Prince Oshisako no Hikohito no Ōe ==See also== * {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}} * {{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=List of emperors of Japan|1=List of emperors of Japan}} * {{ill|Imperial cult|en|Imperial cult|qid=Q3271557|lt=Imperial cult|1=Imperial cult}} ==Notes== {{Reflist|qq=}} ==References== * [[William George Aston|Aston, William George.]] (1896). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ ''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697''.] London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner. {{OCLC|448337491|qq=}} * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0|qq=}}; {{OCLC|251325323|qq=}} * Hopkins, Donald R. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=z2zMKsc1Sn0C&q=The+greatest+killer ''The Greatest Killer''.] Chicago: University of Chicago Press. {{ISBN|9780226351667|qq=}}; {{ISBN|9780226351681|qq=}}; {{OCLC|49305765|qq=}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan''.] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887|qq=}} * {{ill|Isaac Titsingh|en|Isaac Titsingh|qid=Q332779|lt=Titsingh, Isaac.|1=Isaac Titsingh}} (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''] (''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''). Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691|qq=}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] 11 York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5|qq=}}; {{OCLC|59145842|qq=}} {{S-start|qq=}} {{s-reg|qq=}} {{s-bef|before={{ill|Emperor Kinmei|en|Emperor Kinmei|qid=Q329792|lt=Emperor Kinmei|1=Emperor Kinmei}}|qq=}} {{s-ttl|title={{ill|List of emperors of Japan|en|List of emperors of Japan|qid=Q188210|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=List of emperors of Japan}}:
Bidatsu|years=572–585|qq=}} {{s-aft|after={{ill|Emperor Yōmei|en|Emperor Yōmei|qid=Q349281|lt=Emperor Yōmei|1=Emperor Yōmei}}|qq=}} {{S-end|qq=}} {{Emperors of Japan|qq=}} [[en:Emperor Bidatsu]] [[ar:الإمبراطور بيداتسو]] [[arz:الامبراطور بيداتسو]] [[azb:بیداتسو (ایمپیراتور)]] [[cs:Bidacu]] [[de:Bidatsu]] [[es:Bidatsu Tennō]] [[fa:امپراتور بیداتسو]] [[fi:Bidatsu]] [[fr:Bidatsu]] [[id:Kaisar Bidatsu]] [[it:Bidatsu]] [[ja:敏達天皇]] [[ka:ბიდაცუ]] [[ko:비다쓰 천황]] [[mr:बिदात्सु]] [[nl:Bidatsu]] [[pl:Bidatsu]] [[pt:Bidatsu]] [[ru:Император Бидацу]] [[sh:Car Bidatsu]] [[simple:Emperor Bidatsu]] [[sl:Cesar Bidacu]] [[sv:Bidatsu]] [[th:จักรพรรดิบิดัตสึ]] [[tl:Emperador Bidatsu]] [[tr:Bidatsu]] [[uk:Імператор Бідацу]] [[uz:Imperator Bidatsu]] [[vi:Thiên hoàng Bidatsu]] [[yo:Emperor Bidatsu]] [[zh:敏达天皇]] [[zh-min-nan:Bidatu Thian-hông]] [[zh-yue:敏達天皇]] {{wikidata link|Q316637}} [[Category:538 births]] [[Category:585 deaths]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese monarchs]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Deaths from smallpox in Japan]] [[Category:Emperors of Japan]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
